head_coach.name: Dan Quinn
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Pete Carroll tree; aggressive 4-3 hybrid base with Cover 3/Cover 1 core, evolved to incorporate multiple fronts, man coverage, and post-snap disguises. Values versatile, dual-role defenders. CEO approach in Year 2+, delegating playcalling to coordinators.

offensive_coordinator.name: David Blough
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Hybrid: Kingsbury Air Raid + Ben Johnson creativity + Kevin O'Connell West Coast; shifting to pro-style under-center system with play-action and 12 personnel emphasis

defensive_coordinator.name: Daronte Jones
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Brian Flores (Belichick tree) blitz-heavy system blended with Vance Joseph/Lou Anarumo zone-match principles; Vikings 3-4 multiple front

offense.scheme_family: Pro-style hybrid (transitioning from Air Raid/spread to under-center, play-action-heavy system with Ben Johnson influence)
offense.base_personnel: 11/12 (shifting toward more 12 personnel with 2-TE sets; Sinnott/Bates as dual-threat TEs)
offense.run_scheme: Zone-based with more under-center power/gap concepts; moving away from pure shotgun spread run game of Kingsbury era. Play-action and bootleg married to run game.
offense.pass_concepts: Play-action heavy off under-center looks, bootleg concepts, vertical shots, West Coast timing routes. Moving away from Kingsbury's no-huddle/Air Raid quick game. RPO likely reduced in favor of traditional play-action.
offense.rpo_usage: low-to-moderate (major departure from Kingsbury's shotgun-heavy RPO approach; Blough emphasizes under-center play-action instead)
offense.te_usage: Elevated priority: 12 personnel emphasis with TEs used as inline blockers AND move pieces in play-action. Sinnott projected as move TE/FB hybrid, Bates as inline blocker. Hired Ben Steele (developed Trey McBride) as TE coach. Major TE need in draft/FA.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow lean with receiving component; Quinn wants physical run game to control clock. Anthony Lynn still coaching RBs. Back expected to catch passes in play-action-based scheme.

defense.alignment: Hybrid/multiple; Jones stated 'multiple, 3-4, 4-3, they're just numbers.' Expected to install Flores-style 3-4 base with stand-up OLBs as primary edge rushers, but will use even-front looks situationally. Quinn hinted at Daron Payne playing 3-4 DE and nose tackle.
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up (Flores/Vikings model: OLBs line up on DL 92%+ of snaps, wide alignment, but also drop into coverage ~20% of time). Current roster lacks true 3-4 OLBs; major draft/FA need at edge.
defense.coverage_base: Zone-heavy with quarters (Cover 4) family as base; fire zone blitzes behind zone coverage rather than man. Flores influence: league-high zone rate (~76%) paired with heavy blitz rate. Jones also brings Vance Joseph zone-match principles. Quinn's Cover 3/Cover 1 background provides supplementary looks.
defense.blitz_tendency: heavy (Flores/Vikings led NFL in blitz rate every year: 51.5% in 2023, 38.9% in 2024, ~48.7% in 2025. Jones expected to replicate aggressive blitz philosophy. Fire zone blitzes with zone coverage behind them, not pure man.)
defense.lb_role: ILBs cover/run; OLBs are primary edge rushers who also drop into coverage. Flores scheme requires OLBs that can both rush and cover. Vikings OLBs dropped into coverage ~20% of the time while primarily rushing from wide alignments. Versatility is paramount.
defense.safety_usage: Hybrid/versatile: Flores scheme uses safety (Harrison Smith type) as blitzer, slot cover man, and robber. Josh Metellus-type nickel/safety hybrid critical to scheme. Quinn covets 'do-everything' safety (Caleb Downs archetype): deep-third, robber, pattern-match, and blitz roles.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Blough's new offense is explicitly moving toward more 12 personnel with play-action married to the run game. Washington hired Ben Steele (who developed Trey McBride's record-setting 126-catch season) as TE coach. The team has a 'giant hole at TE1' with Ertz departing. Blough and passing game coordinator David Raih bring West Coast and Air Raid receiving TE concepts. Sinnott is projected as a move TE/FB hybrid used on play-action releases. A receiving TE who can also block inline fits perfectly—the scheme creates situations where the TE functions as a primary passing weapon off play-action, not just an inline blocker.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Jones is expected to install the Flores-style 3-4 multiple front where OLBs are the primary edge rushers. In Minnesota, the OLBs (Greenard, Van Ginkel, Turner) lined up on the defensive line on 92%+ of snaps in wide alignments, functioning as de facto edge rushers, but were also asked to drop into coverage ~20% of the time. Quinn hinted at the transition by discussing Payne's versatility as a 3-4 DE/NT. Washington currently lacks true 3-4 OLBs and needs to acquire them. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits AND enough athleticism to drop into coverage gains massive value for this scheme. The Flores system demands edge players who are technically OLBs but function primarily as rushers—the textbook LB-to-pass-rusher value inflection.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Blough's under-center, play-action-heavy scheme values RBs who can sell the run fake and then release into routes. Quinn wants a physical, clock-controlling run game (the reason Kingsbury was fired), so the primary role is still as a runner. However, the Ben Johnson influence and West Coast passing concepts create checkdown and play-action wheel/flat routes for the RB. Not a pure receiving back scheme, but dual-threat ability is valued more than in the Kingsbury shotgun system where the RB was primarily a runner.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: The Flores defensive system is built around safety versatility. In Minnesota, Josh Metellus functioned as a hybrid nickel/safety/blitzer, and Harrison Smith toggled between deep-third, robber, and blitz roles. Jones will bring this philosophy to Washington. Quinn has explicitly coveted a 'do-everything' safety (the Caleb Downs archetype was specifically mentioned in draft analysis tied to Jones). Washington's safety room is thin (Quan Martin, Percy Butler), making this a major need. A safety who can play in the box, cover the slot, blitz off the edge, and rotate to deep coverage gains enormous value in this scheme—it's the linchpin position for the Flores fire zone blitz packages.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Washington's 2026 defense under Daronte Jones is transitioning from Quinn's traditional 4-3 to a Flores-style multiple 3-4 with heavy blitz tendencies and zone coverage. This creates two massive draft value inflections: (1) EDGE/OLB prospects who can rush AND drop into coverage are worth premium prices—the scheme demands stand-up OLBs who line up wide but can drop into fire zone coverage 15-20% of the time, making tweener LB/EDGE prospects (Arvell Reese, Sonny Styles types) significantly more valuable. Washington currently lacks these pieces entirely. (2) Hybrid safeties who can blitz, play slot, and rotate to deep thirds are scheme-critical—a Caleb Downs-type safety is arguably the most impactful non-QB pick for this defense. On offense, Blough's shift to under-center play-action with 12 personnel elevates receiving TEs dramatically—Washington has a glaring TE1 need and hired the coach who developed Trey McBride. A TE like Kenyon Sadiq with elite receiving traits would function as a primary weapon, not just a role player. RBs with receiving chops get a moderate boost from the play-action/boot scheme but the primary need is still a physical runner. The no-huddle Air Raid era is over; Washington now values pro-style, under-center versatility across all positions.
